nativeRange.habitat: Sheltered rocky reefs, commonly found among sponges, soft corals, and algae at depths of 1–15 m

diet.notes: An ambush predator that feeds primarily on fish and crustaceans; tends to lunge and swallow prey whole. Newly acquired specimens generally need live saltwater feeder shrimp or small ghost shrimp to initiate a feeding response; many individuals can eventually be weaned onto frozen meaty foods such as silversides or frozen shrimp offered on a feeding stick. Overfeeding should be avoided, as satiated individuals have been known to refuse food for extended periods.

goodWith: larger docile fish that cannot fit in the angler's mouth
non-aggressive fish of similar or larger size
avoidWith: fish small enough to be swallowed whole
conspecifics unless well-fed and closely monitored

summary: A master of camouflage, the Wartskin Angler uses a worm-like lure to ambush prey. Generally considered reef-safe with caution; best kept with tankmates too large to swallow. Feeding can require patience initially.
careNotes:
The Wartskin Angler (Antennarius maculatus) is generally considered an intermediate-level species due primarily to its specialized feeding requirements rather than water-quality demands — it tends to be hardy once established. The species is found solitary among sponges and soft corals on sheltered Indo-Pacific reefs, and tends to remain nearly motionless for extended periods, making it well-suited to smaller aquariums despite its predatory nature. It has a remarkable chameleonic ability to change color to blend with surrounding substrates, and is sold in a variety of color morphs including pink, yellow, white, orange, and black. Specimens are best kept species-only or with fish and invertebrates too large to be consumed; a newly introduced specimen has been known to consume tankmates significantly larger than expected. A tightly fitting lid is advisable as individuals have occasionally been observed leaving the water. Live rock with sponge growth, caves, and coral ledges provide natural perching and hunting habitat.

Cross-source note on temperature: LiveAquaria recommends 72–78°F (22–26°C), while FishBase wild-habitat data records 25.2–29.3°C in the Indo-Pacific. The values used here (23–28°C) represent an averaged range; aquarists in warmer reef systems (26–28°C) report good results. The 22°C lower bound from LiveAquaria may reflect temperature tolerance rather than the species' preferred wild range.

Cross-source note on adult size: LiveAquaria lists 4 inches (~10 cm) while FishBase records a scientific maximum of 15 cm TL. The 15 cm figure is the documented wild maximum; most aquarium specimens are sold and remain at 5–10 cm.
breedingNotes: Oviparous; eggs are enclosed in a ribbon-like egg raft of gelatinous mucus. Breeding in captivity has been reported but is considered uncommon; detailed captive breeding documentation is limited.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (15 cm TL), temperature range (25.2–29.3°C), native range (Indo-West Pacific), and taxonomy (Antennariidae / Lophiiformes). Temperature range differs from LiveAquaria (22–26°C vs 25–29°C); averaged to 23–28°C. Size differs (10 cm vs 15 cm); used FishBase scientific maximum. Reef2Reef wiki returned 403; BlueZoo had no species page; TFHMagazine returned 404 — FishBase used as secondary.

fish.breedingNotes: Oviparous with gelatinous egg raft; captive spawning reported but uncommon and difficult to replicate reliably.
